bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure ‚Äî feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: Qwik Feature Folders ‚Äî Structure That Works With the Optimizer
bytes.body:
Qwik's optimizer splits code at `$` boundaries ‚Äî your folder structure directly determines bundle granularity. Group by feature under `src/components/`, colocating the `component$`, its CSS module, and tests in one directory. Route-level features belong in `src/routes/` alongside their `layout.tsx`, `index.tsx`, and `plugin@` middleware files.

The tricky part is barrel exports. Qwik's optimizer traces import graphs to build lazy chunks. Wide barrels that re-export across multiple `$` boundaries force the optimizer to merge chunks that should stay separate, bloating your initial load. Keep each `index.ts` barrel shallow ‚Äî only re-export the public `component$` and its prop types.

Never barrel-export `useTask$`, `useResource$`, or `server$` functions alongside UI components. These live in distinct lazy boundaries; merging them in one barrel causes the optimizer to pull server-only or deferred code into the same chunk as eagerly-loaded UI. One safe rule: if the symbol ends with `$`, don't let it escape through a shared barrel unless it's the component itself.

bytes.topic: Component Design ‚Äî single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: Qwik Component Design ‚Äî Single Responsibility, the 150-Line Rule, and Separating Logic from JSX
bytes.body:
Qwik's optimizer serializes component boundaries into QRLs ‚Äî the more logic packed into one `component$()`, the coarser the lazy-loading granularity. Single responsibility isn't just clean code here; it's a performance contract.

The 150-line rule has real teeth in Qwik: a bloated `component$()` forces the optimizer to bundle a larger resumable chunk. Split logic from presentation by extracting custom hooks ‚Äî named `use*` functions that compose `useSignal()`, `useTask$()`, and `useComputed$()` ‚Äî and keep `component$()` responsible only for returning JSX.

This separation also sharpens Qwik's fine-grained reactivity. When a `useSignal()` lives inside a dedicated hook, only subscribers to that signal re-render on change. Mixing data-fetching, transformation, and rendering in one `component$()` creates unnecessary subscription coupling across the tree.

`useVisibleTask$()` especially should never cohabit with layout markup. Extract client-only effects into a dedicated hook, leaving the `component$()` body a thin JSX shell. The optimizer can then tree-shake and defer each unit independently ‚Äî the payoff Qwik's architecture is built around.

bytes.topic: State Management ‚Äî local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: Qwik State Management ‚Äî Local Signal, Lifted Prop, Context Store, or External?
bytes.body:
Qwik's reactivity is signal-based, so state placement rules differ from VDOM frameworks. `useSignal<T>()` gives a primitive reactive value; `useStore<T>()` gives a deep-reactive object proxy ‚Äî both scope to the current component and serialize into its QRL boundary on SSR.

Lifting in Qwik is uniquely zero-cost: pass the signal ref itself as a prop. The child subscribes directly to the signal ‚Äî the parent component does not re-execute. No cascading renders, unlike React's lifted state.

For sub-tree sharing, use `createContextId<T>()` + `useContextProvider()` in the ancestor and `useContext()` in consumers. Qwik's context stores are resumable ‚Äî serialized in the SSR payload, individual components resume only when they interact with that slice.

External stores (Nanostores, module-level `useStore()`) suit cross-route or singleton state. Caution: module-level stores live outside Qwik's serialization boundary and break resumability ‚Äî they must be re-initialized on the client. For anything SSR-sensitive, always prefer context.

Decision ladder: `useSignal` (local atom) ‚Üí lifted signal prop (sibling coordination) ‚Üí context store (sub-tree) ‚Üí external store (app-wide or cross-route).

bytes.topic: Performance ‚Äî memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: Qwik Memoization ‚Äî useComputed$ Helps, useCallback Doesn't Exist (By Design)
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumable architecture makes React-style memoization mostly irrelevant. There's no useMemo, no useCallback, and no memo() HOC ‚Äî because Qwik components don't re-execute as functions on state change. Only the fine-grained reactive scopes that depend on a changed signal re-run, surgically.

The one memoization primitive you'll reach for is useComputed$(). It creates a derived readonly signal that auto-tracks its signal dependencies ‚Äî no dependency array required. It recomputes lazily, only when a tracked signal changes and its value is actually read.

Where it helps: expensive multi-signal derivations like filtered lists, aggregated stats, or formatted currency. Where it hurts: trivial expressions. Wrapping `signal.value + 's'` in useComputed$() adds a subscription graph node for zero benefit ‚Äî just interpolate it inline in JSX.

Event handlers are $() QRLs ‚Äî serialized, lazy-loaded, and stable by nature. The concept useCallback solves in React doesn't exist as a problem in Qwik.

Rule of thumb: reach for useComputed$() when the derivation is expensive or consumed by multiple JSX expressions. Otherwise, read signals directly.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching ‚Äî server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: Qwik Data Fetching ‚Äî Server State vs Client State and Where TanStack Query Fits
bytes.body:
Qwik separates data fetching at the architecture level, not just the API level. `routeLoader$` runs exclusively on the server ‚Äî its result is serialized into HTML and resumed on the client via Qwik's resumability model. No re-execution, no client waterfall. This is your server state primitive.

For reactive client state, `useResource$` is the idiomatic answer. It accepts a `track` callback to declare signal dependencies ‚Äî when tracked signals change, the resource re-runs automatically. The `cleanup` callback handles request cancellation via AbortController, solving the stale-request problem that TanStack Query handles with `queryKey` invalidation.

Where TanStack Query gives you a global `queryClient` with `staleTime` and cache invalidation, Qwik's `routeLoader$` cache is per-request and tied to the routing layer. Mutations flow through `routeAction$`, which triggers automatic `routeLoader$` revalidation on the current route ‚Äî no manual `invalidateQueries` needed.

Reach for `routeLoader$` for any data needed at page load. Use `useResource$` for user-driven, signal-dependent fetches like search or filters. Only introduce TanStack Query when you need cross-route cache sharing or background refetch strategies ‚Äî Qwik's built-ins cover the vast majority of real-world cases without the extra dependency.

bytes.topic: Forms ‚Äî controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: Qwik Forms ‚Äî Controlled vs Uncontrolled, and the routeAction$ + zod$ Pattern
bytes.body:
Qwik inverts the React Hook Form mental model entirely. Instead of client-managed state wired to validation libraries, reach for `routeAction$` + `zod$` ‚Äî Qwik City's server-action primitive that validates before hydration even begins.

Controlled inputs ‚Äî driven by `useSignal<string>('')` ‚Äî make sense for real-time UI feedback like character counters or dependent selects. For everything else, uncontrolled native submission via `<Form action={action}>` is idiomatic Qwik: zero JS downloaded for the form, no event listeners registered until interaction.

`routeAction$` takes a `zod$` validator as its second argument. The schema runs server-side; `action.value.fieldErrors` surfaces per-field errors back to the component without shipping any validation code to the client bundle. Progressive enhancement is implicit ‚Äî the form works before the JS chunk loads.

For complex client-side flows requiring `onChange` validation, use `modular-forms` ‚Äî the Qwik-native RHF equivalent. It integrates directly with `useSignal` and respects Qwik's resumability: only the dirty field's signal invalidates, leaving the rest of the form tree untouched.

bytes.topic: TypeScript ‚Äî prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: Qwik Prop Typing ‚Äî Discriminated Unions for Variants, Zero `any`
bytes.body:
Qwik's `component$` accepts a generic type parameter ‚Äî use it. When a component has visual variants (Button, Badge, Alert), reach for discriminated unions instead of a pile of optional props typed as `string | undefined`.

Each union member narrows the shape: an `'icon'` variant requires `icon: string`, while a `'static'` variant omits `onClick$` entirely. TypeScript's control-flow narrowing then works inside the render logic ‚Äî after checking `props.variant === 'icon'`, `props.icon` is fully typed with no assertions needed.

Avoid `any` entirely. If a prop varies by variant, it belongs in the union, not as `prop?: any`. Qwik serializes props across the server/client boundary, so loose types hide serialization mismatches until runtime.

One critical Qwik caveat: event handler props use the `$` suffix convention and must be typed as `QRL<() => void>`, not the plain function type `() => void`. The TypeScript compiler won't flag the difference, but Qwik's optimizer will silently fail to lazy-load the handler on hydration. Import `QRL` from `@builder.io/qwik` and type every serialized callback explicitly.

bytes.topic: Error Handling ‚Äî error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: Qwik Error Boundaries ‚Äî Per-Route Isolation with Resource State Handling
bytes.body:
Qwik City's file-based router lets you colocate error boundaries by dropping an `error.tsx` inside any route segment ‚Äî it catches unhandled throws from `routeLoader$` and `routeAction$` scoped to that subtree, leaving sibling routes unaffected.

For async data inside components, `useResource$` paired with the `<Resource>` component gives you first-class loading, error, and empty states without extra flags. The `<Resource>` JSX primitive accepts three render props: `onPending` streams a skeleton while the server serializes the resource, `onRejected` receives the thrown Error object, and `onResolved` receives the resolved value. Crucially, these states are tracked at the signal level ‚Äî only the affected DOM subtree re-renders on transition, not the whole route.

If a `routeLoader$` throws inside a layout, the nearest `error.tsx` boundary catches it; if none exists in the layout chain, Qwik City falls back to the root `src/routes/error.tsx`. For expected empty states (zero results), check inside `onResolved` rather than treating them as errors ‚Äî throwing for empty data pollutes your error telemetry and bypasses the normal render path.

bytes.topic: Testing ‚Äî test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: Qwik Testing ‚Äî Test What Users See, Not What Signals Hold
bytes.body:
Qwik's lazy execution model makes testing implementation details especially risky ‚Äî signals serialize across resumability boundaries, meaning what a signal holds during a test may differ from what it holds after browser resumption. The fix: test rendered output, not internal state.

`createDOM()` from `@builder.io/qwik/testing` gives you a headless DOM with `render`, `screen`, and `userEvent`. Resist the urge to read `signal.value` in assertions. Query the DOM and assert on text content, ARIA attributes, or element presence ‚Äî exactly what a user observes.

This matters for `$`-suffixed handlers too. Qwik serializes `onClick$` closures into QRL chunks; the handler will not be the same function reference across resumption. Asserting `onClick$ === fn` will always fail. Assert on the side effect: the DOM mutation.

The same rule applies to stores and computed signals. Prefer `screen.querySelector('[aria-label="count"]')!.textContent` over `store.count`. Behaviour is durable across serialization; internal shape is not.

bytes.topic: Security ‚Äî dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: Qwik Security ‚Äî dangerouslySetInnerHTML, Token Storage, and Input Sanitization
bytes.body:
Qwik's dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses the framework's default escaping ‚Äî user-generated HTML rendered this way is a direct XSS vector. Always sanitize with `isomorphic-dompurify` before passing content to it; DOMPurify runs safely in both server-side (Node) and browser contexts where Qwik resumes.

For token storage, never use `localStorage` or `sessionStorage` ‚Äî they're readable by any injected script. Qwik City's `routeAction$` and `routeLoader$` run server-side and expose `RequestEventBase`, where `cookie.set()` lets you issue HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies. These tokens are entirely invisible to client JavaScript, making them safe across Qwik's resumability boundary.

Input sanitization belongs in `routeAction$` using `zod$` ‚Äî this binds the schema directly to the action at the type level, rejecting malformed payloads before they reach business logic. Because Qwik's resumability means `server$` functions can re-execute on navigation, treat every server entry point as untrusted. Validate at each boundary, not just on initial page load.

bytes.topic: Code Quality ‚Äî ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: Qwik Code Quality ‚Äî ESLint Plugin, $ Naming Contracts, and the 50-Line Rule
bytes.body:
Qwik's optimizer is pattern-driven: it identifies resumable boundaries by the `$` suffix. Use the wrong name and the optimizer silently skips the extraction ‚Äî no build error, just a broken bundle. The React `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` doesn't apply here; Qwik has its own: `eslint-plugin-qwik`. It enforces the `$` naming contract (flagging misnamed lazy functions), warns when `useTask$` or `useVisibleTask$` are called outside a `component$`, and catches inline arrow functions passed to `onClick$` that aren't wrapped in `$()`. Install via `pnpm add -D eslint-plugin-qwik` and extend `plugin:qwik/recommended`.

For naming: every function crossing a resumption boundary must end in `$`. Custom hook conventions follow the same rule ‚Äî `useCounter$`, not `useCounter`. For the 50-line limit, scope it to `component$` bodies using `max-lines-per-function`. A bloated `component$` expands the serialized QRL closure, increasing the resumption payload on the client. Extract heavy logic into standalone `$`-wrapped helpers or split into child `component$` exports ‚Äî each becomes its own lazy chunk.

bytes.topic: Accessibility ‚Äî semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: Qwik Accessibility ‚Äî Semantic HTML First, useVisibleTask$ for Focus, ARIA as a Last Resort
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability model changes how you think about accessibility. Since components resume rather than hydrate, focus management tied to useEffect patterns from other frameworks doesn't apply ‚Äî use useVisibleTask$ instead, which runs exclusively in the browser after the component is visible in the DOM.

Start with semantic HTML: <button> over <div onClick$>, <nav> over <div role='navigation'>, <dialog> over a custom overlay div. These carry implicit ARIA roles, keyboard semantics, and browser-native behavior for free.

When HTML falls short ‚Äî a custom combobox, a focus trap inside a modal ‚Äî use useVisibleTask$ with track() to respond to signal changes and imperatively manage focus. Avoid useTask$ for DOM focus: it runs in both SSR and browser contexts, so element.focus() will throw server-side.

Add ARIA only at the boundary. aria-modal='true' on <dialog> aids screen readers in certain agents. aria-expanded on a custom trigger should be bound directly to the signal driving it ‚Äî Qwik's fine-grained reactivity keeps the attribute in sync automatically without manual bookkeeping. Never add role='button' to a div when a real <button> works.

bytes.topic: Internationalization ‚Äî never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: Qwik i18n ‚Äî qwik-speak, Intl API, and Why You Can't Just Drop in react-i18next
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability changes how i18n works. Unlike React where react-i18next hooks re-execute on hydration, Qwik serializes component state into HTML ‚Äî translations must be available at SSR time and survive resumption without re-fetching. Use `qwik-speak` (the official Qwik i18n library), which integrates with Qwik's serialization pipeline via `QwikSpeakProvider` at the root.

Never hardcode strings. `useTranslate()` returns a `t()` function that resolves keys from locale JSON files, with inline fallbacks via the `@@` syntax: `t('nav.home@@Home')`. Interpolation is first-class: `t('price@@Price: {{val}}', { val })`.

For dates and numbers, use the `Intl` API directly. `useLocale()` from qwik-speak exposes the active locale object ‚Äî pass its `lang` property (e.g., `'en-US'`) straight into `Intl.NumberFormat` or `Intl.DateTimeFormat`. This keeps formatting logic off the server bundle and executes lazily in the browser.

Locale switching updates a signal, so only components that read from it re-render ‚Äî Qwik's fine-grained reactivity means zero full-tree re-renders on language change. Pair this with per-route lazy-loaded translation chunks to avoid shipping all locales upfront.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals ‚Äî LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: Qwik Web Vitals ‚Äî How Resumability and Component Patterns Shape LCP, CLS, and INP
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability means no hydration replay on load ‚Äî the DOM is already interactive without re-executing component trees. But your component patterns still directly determine Core Web Vitals scores.

**LCP:** `useVisibleTask$` with `strategy: 'document-ready'` runs JS eagerly, competing with paint. The default `strategy: 'intersection-observer'` defers execution until the element is visible ‚Äî always prefer it for non-critical widgets. For hero content, stream it via `routeLoader$` so the LCP element lands in SSR HTML with zero JS dependency.

**CLS:** Qwik skips client-side re-rendering of SSR'd nodes, eliminating hydration-induced DOM mutations entirely. The remaining risk is signal-driven renders: if a `useSignal` update changes layout dimensions before the value resolves, reserve space upfront with CSS `min-height` or `aspect-ratio`.

**INP:** `$`-suffixed handlers (e.g., `onClick$`) are QRL lazy-loaded on first interaction, keeping the main thread idle during load. The trap is synchronous heavy work inside those handlers ‚Äî it still blocks the frame. Offload expensive logic to `useTask$` with `track` dependencies, or wrap in a QRL-isolated function so Qwik can code-split it independently.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks ‚Äî cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: Qwik Memory Leaks ‚Äî Cleanup with useVisibleTask$, AbortController, and removeEventListener
bytes.body:
Qwik's `useVisibleTask$` runs exclusively in the browser after a component becomes visible ‚Äî but without explicit cleanup, subscriptions and fetches outlive the component. Unlike React's `useEffect`, Qwik does not use a return value for teardown. Instead, the `{ cleanup }` destructured argument accepts a callback that fires when the task re-runs or the component is destroyed.

For event listeners, always pair `addEventListener` with `removeEventListener` inside `cleanup`. For async operations, `AbortController` is your cancellation primitive: pass `signal` to `fetch` and call `controller.abort()` in the cleanup. Qwik's resumability means a component can be hydrated, paused, and re-hydrated ‚Äî leaked listeners accumulate across these transitions in ways React developers don't anticipate.

`useTask$` (server + client) also accepts `{ cleanup }`, useful for tearing down interval timers or reactive subscriptions. However, `useTask$` has no DOM access on the server ‚Äî wrap any `window` or `document` references in an `isServer` guard or move them into `useVisibleTask$` entirely.

Treating cleanup as a first-class concern prevents ghost listeners and aborted-request noise from polluting your DevTools network tab.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets ‚Äî singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: Qwik WebSockets ‚Äî Singleton Connections, Reconnection Logic, and Optimistic UI
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability means `useVisibleTask$` is your entry point for WebSockets ‚Äî it runs client-side only, after the component is visible, without blocking SSR. For a singleton, define the socket at module scope: a `getWS()` helper checks `readyState` (0 = CONNECTING, 1 = OPEN) before creating a new instance, giving you free reconnection guarding.

Inside `useVisibleTask$`, attach `onmessage` to push into a `useSignal<string[]>`, and register `cleanup(() => socket.close())` ‚Äî this only fires on unmount, not on re-renders, so you won't thrash the connection. Qwik's fine-grained reactivity re-renders only the DOM nodes subscribed to that signal, not the whole component.

For optimistic UI, append to `messages.value` before calling `socket.send()`. On an `onerror` or server-sent rejection event, splice the speculative entry out. Because signals are serialized into HTML during SSR, your initial message list can be server-rendered ‚Äî the WebSocket then hydrates into that state with zero loading flicker.

Reconnection backoff lives in `socket.onclose`: schedule `setTimeout(() => reconnect(url), Math.min(30000, delay * 2))` and re-attach all handlers in the callback.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config ‚Äî never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: Qwik Environment Config ‚Äî Validate Env Vars at Startup with Zod
bytes.body:
Qwik distinguishes sharply between server and browser environments ‚Äî and your config strategy must too. Server-side secrets (API keys, DB URLs) live in `process.env` and are only accessible in `routeLoader$`, `server$`, or middleware. Browser-safe values must be prefixed with `PUBLIC_` to be exposed via `import.meta.env`. Hardcoding either is a liability.

Validate your entire env contract at server startup using Zod. Create a `src/env.server.ts` module that parses `process.env` with a Zod schema and throws immediately if any required var is missing or malformed. Import this module in your `plugin.ts` (Qwik City's server entry hook) so validation runs before any route handler executes. For public vars, create a parallel `src/env.public.ts` that reads from `import.meta.env` ‚Äî safe to import in components. Never import `env.server.ts` from component files; Qwik's optimizer won't always catch that boundary violation for you, but your users will.

bytes.topic: API Layer ‚Äî single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: Qwik API Layer ‚Äî One HTTP Client, Server$ Interceptors, No Raw Fetch in Components
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability model serializes component state across server/client boundaries ‚Äî raw fetch calls inside components break this contract and leak request context into client bundles. Centralize all HTTP calls behind server$ functions, which execute exclusively on the server and expose only serializable return values to the client.

Define a single apiFetch helper in lib/api.ts that owns base URL, default headers, and error normalization. Wrap it in server$ exports that inject auth tokens via this.cookie (Qwik City's built-in request context) and implement retry logic before returning. routeLoader$ and routeAction$ in your route files call these server$ exports ‚Äî never fetch directly.

This enforces three guarantees: (1) auth tokens never reach client JS bundles, (2) retry/backoff logic lives in one file, (3) Qwik's lazy-loading $ boundaries stay clean because server$ functions are automatically excluded from client chunks. For useResource$, wrap the call in a server$ as well ‚Äî track() dependencies remain reactive while the network layer stays fully server-isolated. The component never knows a network exists.

bytes.topic: Animation ‚Äî CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: Qwik Animations ‚Äî CSS Signals, Motion One, and prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability model means DOM manipulation belongs in `useVisibleTask$`, not a lifecycle hook. For simple state-driven styles, signal-driven class toggling is zero-overhead: bind a class to a signal and let CSS transitions do the work ‚Äî no JS animation loop, no hydration cost.

For complex sequences ‚Äî staggered lists, spring physics, keyframe choreography ‚Äî reach for `@motionone/dom`. Framer Motion is React-only; Motion One is its framework-agnostic sibling and integrates cleanly with Qwik because it operates directly on DOM elements, not a virtual DOM.

The critical pattern: always gate imperative animation behind a `prefers-reduced-motion` check. Since `useVisibleTask$` is guaranteed browser-only, `window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches` is safe here ‚Äî return early and rely on CSS fallbacks instead.

Use `track()` inside `useVisibleTask$` to reactively re-trigger animations when a signal changes. This replaces React's dependency array. Never put imperative DOM animation in `onClick$` handlers expecting a ref ‚Äî the ref may not be resolved at call time without `useVisibleTask$` guarding it.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns ‚Äî toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: Qwik Global UI Patterns ‚Äî Toast, Modal, and Confirm Dialogs Without Hydration Cost
bytes.body:
Qwik's resumability means global UI state ‚Äî toasts, modals, dialogs ‚Äî must be serializable from day one. The right pattern is a `createContextId` store provided at the root layout via `useContextProvider`, consumed anywhere with `useContext`.

For a toast system, create a `ToastCtx` holding `{ items: ToastItem[] }`. In `root.tsx`, initialize with `useStore` and call `useContextProvider`. Any leaf component calls `useContext(ToastCtx)` and mutates directly. Wrap mutations in `$()` to keep them lazy-loadable QRLs ‚Äî Qwik won't eagerly download the handler until invoked.

Modal state follows the same pattern: a `ModalCtx` with `open: boolean` and `payload: unknown`. For confirmation dialogs, encapsulate into a `useConfirm` hook returning a `confirm$` QRL. Internally, it sets a pending `useSignal<Deferred>`, renders the dialog bound to that signal, and resolves on user action.

Critically, because Qwik serializes context stores into the HTML stream during SSR, this state survives resumption with zero re-execution. Your entire global overlay layer adds no hydration penalty ‚Äî the store is already live the moment the user interacts.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization ‚Äî tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: Qwik Bundle Optimization ‚Äî Tree-Shaking, Lazy Segments, and Bundle Analysis
bytes.body: Qwik's optimizer automatically splits your app into fine-grained lazy chunks via its `$` suffix convention ‚Äî every `component$`, `useTask$`, and event handler becomes its own separate segment. But you can push further. Heavy third-party dependencies inside components are only downloaded when that component is resumed, so wrapping costly libraries in `component$` keeps them out of the initial load entirely. Use `useVisibleTask$` instead of `useTask$` for non-critical side effects ‚Äî it defers execution until the element is visible, preventing unnecessary hydration work. For bundle analysis, run `npm run build` and inspect `dist/build/` ‚Äî Qwik emits a `q-manifest.json` that maps every symbol to its chunk. Pair this with `vite-bundle-visualizer` by adding it to your `vite.config.ts` plugins. Avoid barrel files (`index.ts` re-exports) in shared modules ‚Äî Vite's tree-shaker struggles with them and Qwik's optimizer can't split what Vite doesn't isolate. Prefer direct imports to keep each segment tight.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates ‚Äî Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: Qwik Quality Gates ‚Äî Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, and tsc --noEmit That Actually Catch Bugs
bytes.body:
Qwik's optimizer serializes component closures into QRLs at build time ‚Äî which means type errors that a browser would surface at runtime get baked silently into your bundle if you skip static checks. Running `tsc --noEmit` in CI is non-negotiable: it catches invalid `Signal<T>` assignments, mismatched `QRL<() => void>` vs `PropFunction<() => void>` props, and broken `useResource$` return types before they reach the optimizer.

For pre-commit gates, pair Husky with lint-staged scoped to `*.{ts,tsx}`. Always include `eslint-plugin-qwik` in your ESLint config ‚Äî it flags illegal `$`-boundary crossings (e.g., capturing non-serializable values inside `useTask$`) that TypeScript alone won't catch. Prettier should run first in lint-staged so ESLint isn't diffing against unformatted code.

In your GitHub Actions CI step, run `tsc --noEmit` before `vite build` ‚Äî the Qwik Vite plugin won't abort on type errors by default, so the build can succeed while producing broken QRL references. Treat the type check as a hard gate, not a warning.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production ‚Äî Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: Qwik Production Monitoring ‚Äî Sentry, Web Vitals, and Source Maps
bytes.body:
`@sentry/qwik` hooks into Qwik City's router at the signal level ‚Äî route errors and server `routeLoader$` exceptions are captured automatically without wrapping every handler. Initialize the client SDK inside `useVisibleTask$` in `src/root.tsx` so it only runs post-resumption, never during SSR. Keep `tracesSampleRate` low (0.1‚Äì0.2); Qwik's fine-grained lazy loading generates many small transactions that inflate quota fast.

For source maps, set `build: { sourcemap: true }` in `vite.config.ts` and add `@sentry/vite-plugin` ‚Äî it uploads maps to Sentry after each build and strips them from the public bundle. Point `sourcemaps.assets` at `./dist/build/**`; Qwik outputs chunks there, not a flat `assets/` dir.

Web Vitals: Qwik's resumability means TTI is near-zero, but LCP and CLS still need watching. Wire `web-vitals` callbacks in the same `useVisibleTask$` block and push metrics via `Sentry.getCurrentScope().setMeasurement()`. This collapses error tracking and performance observability into one pipeline instead of shipping a separate analytics SDK.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models ‚Äî how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: Qwik Signals vs React/Vue/Svelte/Solid ‚Äî Who Re-Runs and Why
bytes.body:
React re-executes the entire component function on state change ‚Äî useMemo and useCallback are escape hatches bolted on after the fact. Vue tracks property access through Proxy traps at runtime, re-running computed watchers when deps change. Svelte moves reactivity to compile time, emitting targeted DOM updates with no runtime overhead. Solid uses fine-grained signals where only the exact JSX expression reading a signal re-evaluates ‚Äî no component re-render at all.

Qwik starts where Solid leaves off and adds resumability. `useSignal()` creates a serializable signal whose subscription graph is captured on the server and transferred to the client as JSON ‚Äî no hydration, no re-execution. When a signal changes, Qwik surgically updates only the DOM nodes bound to that signal. Crucially, the component's JS is never downloaded unless it contains an event handler that fires. `useComputed$()` memoizes derived values lazily; `useTask$()` runs side effects tracked against signal reads inside the task body. The `$` suffix marks a lazy boundary ‚Äî Qwik splits those closures into separate chunks automatically.
